serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Bak Kut Teh (All Ribs) is approximately 500 g, comprising pork spare and back ribs submerged in herbal pork broth, typically served with steamed white rice and you tiao (fried dough fritters) on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Bak Kut Teh, pork ribs in herbal soup — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO INFOODS SEA composite; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central pork rib and broth entries
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for 'Bak Kut Teh All Ribs' as a restaurant dish. Values are adapted from IMR MyFCD pork rib soup entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA pork broth data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168319, pork spare ribs cooked; FDC ID 172950, pork broth). The all-ribs variant is higher in protein and fat than a standard mixed-cut serving. Values represent a weighted composite per 100 g of the combined dish (ribs + broth as consumed).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of meat-based soups and broths — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate pork soup entries; University of Sydney GI Database reference for meat and broth dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: Bak Kut Teh is not directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI is estimated at 10 (low) based on the very low carbohydrate content of the dish (1.2 g per 100 g, predominantly from soy sauce and trace starch from herbs). Analogous low-carbohydrate meat soups and broths in the University of Sydney GI Database consistently register GI values below 15. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (10 × 6.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.6, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and prolonged broth reduction
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g restaurant serving of Bak Kut Teh (All Ribs) delivers approximately 2100 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce, avoid drinking the broth in full, and limit portion frequency to occasional consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate and glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic load of 0.6 per serving, Bak Kut Teh (All Ribs) has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total meal carbohydrate by accounting for accompanying rice or you tiao.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork ribs and elevated sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (2.6 g per 100 g, approximately 13 g per serving) and high sodium load are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving frequency, choose leaner cuts where available, and avoid consuming the full volume of broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bak Kut Teh (All Ribs) is a traditional Malaysian and Singaporean pork rib soup in which pork spare ribs and back ribs are slow-simmered for several hours in a deeply aromatic herbal broth. The broth is built from a combination of white pepper, garlic, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and a blend of Chinese medicinal herbs including dang gui (angelica root), yu zhu (Solomon's seal rhizome), star anise, cinnamon, and cloves. The 'all ribs' designation indicates that the serving contains exclusively pork rib cuts rather than a mixed offal or organ-meat combination. The dish is characterised by a rich, peppery, slightly sweet and savoury broth with tender, fall-off-the-bone pork ribs. Texturally, the meat is soft and gelatinous from the collagen released during prolonged simmering. Bak Kut Teh is typically served with steamed white rice, you tiao (fried dough fritters), and a side of dark soy sauce with sliced chilli for dipping. It is a popular breakfast and lunch dish in Malaysia and Singapore, with strong cultural roots in the Hokkien Chinese community. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 9.8 g per 100 g) and moderate in fat (7.2 g per 100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 g), making it glycemically benign. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content, derived from soy sauce and broth concentration, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not halal due to pork content.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for Bak Kut Teh (All Ribs) as a specific restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD pork rib soup entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA pork broth data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central pork rib and broth entries. The all-ribs variant introduces additional uncertainty relative to a standard mixed-cut entry. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. The input record incorrectly classified this dish under food_group 'Beverages'; corrected to 'Meat and alternatives' as Bak Kut Teh is a pork rib soup dish, not a beverage. 2. The food_name has been normalised to 'Bak Kut Teh (All Ribs)' as the canonical English name; the original field survey listing name 'A4 Legendary Bak Kut Teh (All Ribs) 發传人全排骨' is retained as an alias. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source entries for pork rib soup. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source entries for this dish. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source entries for pork rib soup. 6. GI is set to 10 (low) as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Bak Kut Teh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The dish is not halal due to pork content. 8. Sodium content is high and clinically significant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management.
